posts.text:
AI governance pulls us in 5 directions: centralized vs. decentralized control, robust vs. minimal enforcement, adaptive vs. enduring rules. How do we balance these tensions? 🤖

Our analysis draws lessons from aviation, climate policy & more 🧵

http://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.5

We examined 4 governance models to understand trade-offs:

✈️ Aviation: Centralized, safety-first, but costly
🌱 Organic food: Decentralized, private-driven, inconsistent
🌍 Climate: Shared, flexible, but weak enforcement
💻 Open-source: Collaborative, adaptive, but fragile

The current AI governance landscape shows clear fragmentation:

• EU centralizing with the AI Act
• US taking decentralized, sector-specific approach
• Monitoring ranges from strict compliance to voluntary audits
• Tech firms dominate decisions while public input is sidelined

The pace of AI development adds another layer of complexity. Generative AI's rapid emergence left policymakers scrambling.

A major crisis—like a deepfake election scandal—could force rapid regulatory responses. Public demand for accountability grows stronger daily.

Our analysis suggests hybrid models that combine:
• Centralized safety oversight (aviation-style)
• Decentralized innovation hubs (open-source approach)
• Broad societal goals (climate policy framework)

Experimentation across jurisdictions will be key.

Why this matters: AI governance will shape not just technology, but society itself. Done right, it could address inequality and climate impact. Done wrong, it risks entrenching power imbalances.

Published in Handbook on the Global Governance of AI.

What trade-offs would you prioritize in AI governance? Centralization or flexibility? Private leadership or public control?

For policy practitioners and governance teams.

@GRAIL_center @purduepolsci #AIGovernance

http://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.5343797

linkedin.text:
AI governance is pulling us in five directions at once, creating unprecedented regulatory challenges. Our new analysis examines how lessons from aviation, climate policy, organic food standards, and open-source software can guide AI governance design.

http://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.5343797

✅ We identified key tensions shaping AI governance:
• Centralized vs. decentralized control
• Robust vs. minimal enforcement mechanisms
• Adaptive vs. enduring regulatory frameworks
• Private leadership vs. shared governance
• Narrow vs. broad societal objectives

➤ Current landscape shows clear fragmentation:
The EU is centralizing through the AI Act while the US pursues decentralized, sector-specific approaches. Monitoring varies from strict compliance requirements to voluntary audits. Tech firms dominate decision-making while public input remains limited.

➤ Our analysis suggests hybrid governance models that combine:
• Centralized safety oversight (aviation-style)
• Decentralized innovation hubs (open-source approach) 
• Broad societal goal integration (climate policy framework)

The stakes couldn't be higher. AI governance will shape not just technology development, but societal outcomes around inequality, climate impact, and democratic participation. Published in the Handbook on the Global Governance of AI.

For policy practitioners, governance teams, and researchers working on responsible AI implementation—curious what governance trade-offs you're prioritizing in your work?

GRAIL Center | Purdue University

#AIGovernance #ResponsibleAI #TechPolicy #AIEthics #PolicyResearch #DigitalGovernance

posts.text:
AI governance faces 5 fundamental tensions: centralized vs. decentralized control, robust vs. minimal enforcement, adaptive vs. enduring rules. How do we navigate these trade-offs? 🤖

Our analysis draws lessons from aviation, climate & open-source governance 👇

http://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.534

We examined 4 governance models to understand the possibilities:

✈️ Aviation: Centralized, safety-focused, but expensive
🌱 Organic standards: Decentralized, private-led, inconsistent
🌍 Climate policy: Shared, flexible, weak enforcement
💻 Open-source: Collaborative, adaptive, fragile

Current AI governance shows fragmentation everywhere:

• EU centralizing with AI Act
• US pursuing decentralized approaches
• Monitoring ranges from strict to voluntary
• Tech firms drive decisions while public input lags

The pace of AI development only complicates things further.

Results suggest hybrid models combining:
• Centralized safety oversight
• Decentralized innovation spaces
• Broad societal goal integration

Experimentation across jurisdictions will be essential. Published in Handbook on the Global Governance of AI.

AI governance will shape society—addressing inequality and climate impact if done right, entrenching power imbalances if done wrong.

What governance trade-offs matter most to you? For policy practitioners and researchers.
